As much as she wanted to rush over, she knew he would want her to at least check on the kids first.
"You're not responsible for taking care of his feelings on this, you know?" Fin broke the silence.
She nodded, then realized his eyes were on the road. "Yeah, I do." Her voice was soft. "But we've had 10 years to process. He's had what, 10 minutes?"
"You really think he just found out?" He asked, doubtful, and she shrugged.
"I have no idea. It was the only option that didn't make me want to strangle him, so..."
"Well, look at you picking the path of least resistance for once."
"Oh, shut up." She rolled her eyes at him, laughing. After a moment of silence, she added a rather quiet, "Thanks for coming."
"Always."
When Maureen opened the door, Olivia noticed she looked more timid than normal.
"Whatever you said to Dad must have worked. He's on the phone with Katie. She's on the balcony."
Olivia gave her a warm hug before continuing through to the kitchen.
"Dickie, what the hell?" It slipped out before she caught herself. He was sitting on Elliot's kitchen counter, cursing while his twin cleaned up cuts on his hand.
"The bathroom mirror is a very sore loser. Didn't go down without a fight." The blonde quipped, before turning to hug Liv herself. He grumbled something she didn't quite catch and wouldn't look her in the eyes. Fin noticed immediately and cut right to the chase.
"Ok, someone better start talking."
Both girls sighed and looked at Dickie. Olivia saw Katie and Eli sitting on the deck chairs, both talking into the phone in Katie's hand. She wondered what he was saying, but at least she felt like he was safe. She was too curious about Elliot's oldest son to abandon this conversation yet.
"None of the boys knew." Lizzie blurted out, and Maureen shot her a look. She shrugged as if to say, 'What was I supposed to say?' Then looked back at Olivia. "Sorry." Olivia squeezed her shoulder before moving to her side next to Richard. She decided to busy herself with the first aid kit while they discussed whatever this was. It was a good distraction.
"Okay." She said slowly, prompting them to continue. It was Maureen who spoke next, but it came out in a nervous rush.
"Well, it came on, and I asked Eli to turn it off but of course he had no idea what was happening because he was so young when it happened and by then Richard had picked up on it, and before I could find the damn remote, dad was paying attention and we didn't really realize what had happened till it was over. Katie and Eli were livid, Lizzie had started crying, and I still couldn't find that fucking remote, and it just took us a second to realize Dad and Richard were just standing there...I don't know, in shock or something." She took a breath, and Olivia was still staring at the first aid kit, her hands on the counter now. She had stopped moving altogether. "I thought he was gonna explode, but he wouldn't respond to any of us, and then he just said he had to go, and we assumed it was to go find you. Katie and I were getting ready to go make sure he was ok when this one's brain finally flipped a switch, and he hauled off and punched the mirror -" She stopped suddenly when the glass door slid open, and Eli entered the room. Olivia stepped back a bit from the counter, trying to process everything Maureen had just said, when she suddenly felt arms around her. She jumped at first, but then she realized Eli wasn't letting go, so she just held him tighter. She met Fins' gaze to check in, let him know she was ok, then turned her attention back to the young man in front of her.
"Hey, it's ok." She whispered when she realized he was crying. "Everything's alright."
"You weren't alright." It was the first thing Richard had said out loud since they had arrived.
"I'm here now." She responded before looking around the room. Katie had walked in as well, so they were all staring at her. Fin was still nearby but giving the Stablers their space with her. "You guys, I promise. I'm good."
Lizzie gasped, making all of them jump, and started shooing Eli off of her.
"Oh my god, you just got shot! Dude, get off of her!" Eli jumped back, apologizing, and Olivia put a hand on Lizzie and Eli's shoulders to calm them.
"Seriously, I'm fine."
"She's always fine." Said Fin with a smirk, and she quickly pointed at him.
"How many times do I have to tell you to shut up today?"
"Yes, Captain." She narrowed her eyes at his tone, but at least his smart remarks got a smile out of a few of them.
"Dad said he'll come back if you want." Katie updated the group, but was looking at Olivia. Her brow furrowed, but Katie just shrugged, not willing to offer up anything else. She picked up her phone from the counter and sent a text:
Get your ass home now, Stabler.
What on earth was she supposed to do with this family? She was still very confused, but she had certainly picked up on a few things from Maureen's story. The girls were aware of William Lewis, but the boys had all somehow been kept in the dark. She was too curious not to find out why.
"Fin, could you and Eli maybe clean up the broken glass? Lizzie and I can finish cleaning him up," she motioned to Dickie, "then maybe by then your dad will be home." She offered. Really, she wanted a chance to talk to the older kids on her own, but she hoped to spare Eli's feelings.
Fin nodded, and Eli gave her another, more cautious hug, before retreating. Olivia took over cleaning the last few cuts while the rest of them stared at her quietly.
"You want to tell me what got you so upset?" She tried to make eye contact with him, but he was still very determined to avoid her. Stabler men.
"He was deployed at the time. Hard to reach, so everything was settled before we could talk to him. He got an, um, abridged version of events." Katie offered meekly, and he scoffed.
"Abridged?!" He challenged, looking at his sister. "You three told me some sketchy mother fucker tried to take down Liv and SVU and failed. You said she got hurt. That,"  he pointed to the tv, "that was not 'hurt'." He had tried to use both hands to make finger quotes and winced. Olivia grabbed his arm

losing you
“Uh, sorry man, you’re losing me,” Jermaine said airily, laughing it off. Omi’s tales about his grand Xiaolin adventures were always fantastical, and the kid was always excitable, but this time he was just going too fast and bringing in too much all at once. Made Jermaine’s head spin sometimes. That was the fun of their friendship, though.
“—What?” Omi squeaked out, eyes wide. “I am losing you?!”
“Uh…?” Before Jermaine could even breathe to get out the words “What’s up, Omi?”, Omi had tackled him in a tearful embrace.
“NO! PLEASE! I DO NOT WANT TO LOSE YOU, JERMAINE! I VALUE YOU SO MUCH! I WILL MISS YOU! I…” Omi’s fingers clutched Jermaine’s shirt protectively, stretching out the now-tear-soaked fabric. “I WILL NOT LOSE YOU! I WILL SAVE YOU! TELL ME WHAT IS WRONG, AND I WILL PROTECT YOU!”
“D—Dawg, calm down, it’s a figure of speech! I’m still here!”
Omi’s hold loosened. Slightly. Barely. “…You are? I have not lost you?”
Jermaine put his hands on Omi’s shoulders. “Hey, come up, buck up! You’re tough, right? What’s got you so worked up?”
“Because I do not want to lose you like I lost everyone else!”
“…What?”
Omi lowered his head in shame and buried his face in Jermaine’s shirt. “I… have seen almost all my friends die before, and it was my fault. Master Fung had his soul trapped in the Yin-Yang realm because of a trap that was set to me. I have watched Chase Young sacrifice himself to save me, when we were finally good friends too.” Omi took a breath. “…I am most tired losing everyone I thought I had. Again, and again.”
“—Pause. Can we circle back to the fact that you apparently consider Chase Young one of your closest friends?”
Omi looked up at Jermaine with teary eyes and a complicated expression. “I… We…”
Jermaine sighed. “You know what, we’ll put a pin in that for that for now. Anyway, all those times, what about me? Did I disappear or die or something?” he asked. He didn’t really know the answer, or the context of most of Omi’s rambling, so he just had to cross his fingers and hope for a good answer.
Thankfully, Omi shook his head. Jermaine internally sighed a breath of relief.
“Well, good. I mean, I don’t have the whole story here, but from the sounds of it… you’ve never lost me yet, have you?”
“…No.” Omi sniffled out.
“And you’re not gonna start now. I’m right here.”
“…Really? I have not lost you?”
Jermaine held out a fist. “You ain’t losin’ me. We’re besties, right? You my homeboy.”
“Besties!” Omi tapped his fist against Jermaine’s, and they did their little secret handshake that the Jermaine and Omi had made just for the two of them. “Homeboys! Fam!”
Jermaine chuckled. “Yeah, you got it right! Fam!”

Chapter 1
I woke up to the sound of the pilot announcing the plane landed. As I opened my eyes and looked out the window I saw that after 15 hours the plane had finally stopped moving.
I had arrived in Seoul.
Emotions of curiosity, wonder and a bit of anxiety filled my body. I had left my life back home to pursue a career as a K-pop idol. That was crazy…right? Ever since I got that email from JYP entertainment about a cover video my dance crew did, I hadn’t given much thought to what my new life would look like as a trainee. Will the girls in my group like me? What if I’m not put into a group and have to go solo? What if I don’t even make it past training at all?
I shook my head. I grabbed my backpack and hesitantly made it off the plane. There was time for existential dread later. I became overwhelmed as I took in my new surroundings. People speaking a language I couldn't understand constant movement wherever I looked, like everyone else had their life in order…except me. As I made my way through the airport and waited for my luggage to come around the baggage claim carousel a man dressed in black dress pants, white dress shirt and a black jacket came to stand beside me.
“Miss Y/N?”
I was shaken from my thoughts, startled. How did he know my name?
“Yes?” I replied.
“I’m Kang Seomin from JYP entertainment, I was sent to pick you up from the airport.”
So this is it…my new life begins now. I grabbed my luggage as it came around the baggage carousel and followed Seomin to the parking lot

A Warm Bakushin Morning
Sakura Bakushin O, the self-proclaimed strongest class representative, positions herself firm and proud just beyond the cold iron gates. Her stance is sure, her confident sakura blossom-eyes are locked in beyond the gold horizon, feet planted like a bulwark of the student body that she works day after day to support and make proud. She takes a deep breath, catching a cool breeze that will envelop these grounds like a soft blanket. Its familiarity and gentleness washes over Bakushin, a reminder of days gone by for the Sprint King.
Soon, a mass movement is noticed entering the class representative's sight.
“It is time,” Bakushin thinks, taking a split second to touch the brooch that was around her neck. With her famous wide, bright smile, she hoists the megaphone up to her mouth.
“GOOD BAKUSHIN, TRACEN ACADEMY STUDENTS!!!,” The blaring echo of the megaphone blasts through the incoming wave of umamusume, “This is your class representative, Sakura Bakushin O, welcoming you to another beautiful Bakushin day!” While this earned the usual groans from some students walking into the academy, (as for them, it was too early for such loudness) it did not deter the class representative from the path to success of her more direct approach to the second Proper Manners Initiative.
Initially, the approach was to be more hands-off as per Air Groove's wishes much like the first time. Putting up posters, intercom reminders throughout the day, bulletin board notices… Methods that were unobtrusive to a student's day-by-day. The student council members were wise enough to deduce that a more passive approach was the more likely path to success in this initiative, but much like last time, Bakushin believed in something more up front and rather bombastic.
“WONDERFUL STUDENTS OF TRACEN, today the forecast is sunny skies, gentle breezes, with some gusts! A perfect day for training and a perfect day to BAKUSHIN!”
Her beaming personage continued to greet the incoming students one-by-one, about half of them waved and bowed to the class representative. It was about an hour until the first classes of the day would begin, and for Bakushin, there was no better moment to tackle the Proper Manners Initiative head-on! As Sprint King, she always believed she could make anything happen, the success of this was all but guaranteed for her.
The first couple of minutes was a clean rotation of alternating between megaphone, greetings, and welcoming gestures. It was, as with the previous days, like clockwork for Bakushin. The plan was simple: welcome the students to the new day with her Bakushin energy first thing and awaken them with her boundless energy and enthusiasm! This was followed up in tandem with the Disciplinary Committee who had posted themselves just inside the building entryway to dole out their own reminders of following campus rules and code. Teamwork, the pathway to success.
After a few minutes into the initial wave, a familiar face to the sprint king approached from within the student mass. While the hip-length, straight jet black hair was rather common, the large white crescent cowlick betrayed any sense of subtlety the student would ever have. Calstone Light O, alongside her roommate and fellow sprinter, the knightess Durandal, approached their upperclassman who offered them her morning salutations.
“Good morning fellow disciples of speed! I hope today will bring you a myriad of Bakushin blessings and success!” In response, Durandal bowed forward. Light O’s own bow consisted instead of stiffening her posture the straightest she could like an imperial palace guard.
“Good morning to you as well, Bakushin O-senpai!” The straight-laced Light O greeted first with her signature monotone loudness, Durandal followed up her roommate with a returned greeting as well, more proper and elegant “A fine morning indeed. Thank you senpai, for the blessings.”
“Durandal! I was able to see your recent victory in the Dream series! A fine display of that unique running strategy of yours. I am consistently impressed whenever I witness it!” Bakushin’s compliment was one that sent the blonde knightess internally flustering. Though her outward form showed the stone-faced sternness befitting of a royal knight, Durandal was in fact, very close to breaking into a glimmering smile.
Durandal shook her head as her lips were fighting to twitch upward, “I… I am grateful for the praise, senpai.” she then took to her signature stance, her right arm held over her chest, left arm placed behind her. In terms of Sprinters making their mark, Durandal was a particular one that the sprint king kept her ears open for in regards to her progress even after her career. Before she debuted, none could ever imagine an umamusume making a Chaser’s strategy work in such short distances, but the royal knightess proved that a definite reality due to her acceleration being second-to-none on the sprinter’s field. How befitting of a strategy it was for someone like her too: like a knight commander powerfully charging straight into battle with the front lines for their monarch.
Bakushin then placed her gaze upon Durandal’s roommate with the piercing golden eyes and extended her arm outward to her, “Light O! Your trainer has told me of your improvement on your water running technique! I cannot wait to race across the ocean against you someday!”
Durandal internally sighed, “Not the water running again…” she had only heard of and seen the aftermath of her roommate’s “successful” attempt to run across water one Summer’s day which had involved pulling her trainer in a vinyl boat and the two having to be fished out of the ocean. She knew Bakushin was also capable of this technique, but had hoped that an eventual showdown between the two speed-obsessed umamusume would take place on more… conventional turf than the ebbing and flowing of sea water.
Light O’s face tensed which beget the utter joy she felt at the unexpected praise from her senpai, “I am grateful, Bakushin O-senpai! I’ve been doing my best to improve my control and I truly believe that this time, maximum speed is finally within reach!” Bakushin loved hearing about the improvement of her sprinter peers around her. She may have been the strongest in speed, but what is being the strongest if those around you aren’t?
“--I have also been improving my breathing technique to gather even more energy for speed! Would you like a demonstration?” Light O immediately began to jog in place as fast as she could, kicking up a small cloud of micro debris as her feet shuffled on the concrete, “SUUUUUUUUU– HAAAAAAAAAA–” Light O’s sudden loud bellowing startled both the class representative and Durandal who quickly shut her friend down by loudly clearing her throat. The two noticed that Light O’s antics had caused some surrounding passerbys to bend their path slightly more away from the trio.
“Apologies senpai, apparently Light O has repurposed her breathing technique that was originally meant to calm herself down…” While Light O was cut off from her shouting, she continued to jog in place, her face never breaking from that famed penetrating stare.
The black-haired umamusume was known around campus for her particularly odd, very straightforward and eccentric personality that bled seamlessly into her racing form. Her straightline speed, much like her friend’s acceleration, was without match in short-distances almost to a comical degree. It was as if the sound barrier would break at any moment Light O flew down any straight.
Bakushin let out a hearty laugh as she took to a runner's stance, “No need to apologize on her behalf! I can just feel the energy resonating from her! As one who can rival me in speed, I cannot wait to see how far your Light O speed will take you!” The black-haired umamusume halted her jog while her eyes widened so much that one could see how much they sparkled at the praise.
A thought then came upon Bakushin, “Speaking of Believe, is she not accompanying you two today?” It was an odd sight indeed to not see the key third of the trio today. The two senior underclassmen would inform Bakushin of Believe’s illness that has bedridden her for the moment.
“Ah, please inform her then that I wish her good Bakushin health! Bakushiiin~!” The two sprinters smiled and nodded at the well-wishes for their ill friend, “Now on your way, you two! You do not want to be late for class!”
The reminder from the class representative immediately kicked up Light O’s jog again, “Indeed! Thank you for the reminder, senpai! Haaaaaaaaaaaa~!” she launched herself in the direction of the academy building.
“Light O, calm down or else you’ll collide with a wall again like yesterday!” Durandal called out to Light O as she chased after her. Soon enough, one could faintly hear the loud scolding voice of one Fenomeno echoing from the academy halls.
As the two disappeared into the academy building behind her, the class representative felt a warm hopefulness enveloping her. Ever since the day of her final race, the landscape of sprints had changed. The uptick in new short-distance umamusume amazed the retired sprinter to no end, for however boisterous the class representative was, she couldn’t betray how bashful she felt that her generation brought about this new continuously ongoing crop of sprinter hopefuls, including at one point, the two umamusume that she had just finished talking with.
Returning herself to position, Bakushin hoisted the megaphone up to her mouth again while catching a slightly stronger gust in the process, “GOOD BAKUSHIN MORNING, TRACEN STUDENTS! If you are wearing any sort of headware, please do your best to keep them fastened so they don’t blow away!” That said, she already had seen a student fumbling with her baseball cap out of the corner of her eye. She had managed to give a deep bow to Bakushin in embarrassment after catching her hat.
Bakushin continued, “REMINDER! Registration for next month’s races ends this week! Do not make all your training go to waste! As they say: Punctuality! Timeliness! Bakushiiiiin~!!!” dropping the megaphone from her face, Bakushin let it dangle from her arm strap while she took a drink from a water bottle. As she gulped the water down, an umamusume with similar cherry-blossom eyes approached her just out of view enough to catch her off-guard.
“Your spirit really is endless, isn’t it?” The umamusume chuckled, sending the representative to turn herself to face her.
Bakushin was taken aback by the sudden appearance of her childhood friend, “Chitose!” she blurted as she fumbled the water bottle in her hands.
Brushing to the side her dull purple bangs and fluttering her pinkish eyes, Sakura Chitose O smiled, “How has it been, Bakushin? I’ve been meaning to take you aside during one of these morning greeting sessions of yours.”
Bakushin chuckled to herself as she put down her bottle, “Then you’ve picked the right opportunity! To what do I owe the pleasure of the appearance of an old friend of mine?” Her toothy wide smile was one that Chitose could never forget.
The purple-haired umamusume excitedly began, “Well, Laurel will be returning from her excursion to France really soon, so me and the other Victory Club members are planning a reunion of sorts since it’s been quite a moment since our last get-together!”
The invitation was a little bit of a shock to Bakushin. During their Twinkle careers, Bakushin was the second of her childhood friend group to retire from competition following Sakura Chiyono O. Afterward, while she still did her best to keep up with the remaining active’s respective careers, other reasons had managed to occupy the retiree’s time and attention which included a greater focus on her class representative duties.
“It’s… been so long hasn’t it?” Bakushin mentioned, her tone going uncharacteristically mellow as her face began to deflate, “I apologize, Chitose. I know I haven’t been around as often as I was before–” Chitose lifted an index finger to Bakushin’s face.
“Not another word like that, Bakushin.” Chitose maintained her gentle face as she interrupted her childhood friend’s apology, “It was obvious you had very important reasons that brought your attention away from us. Remember, we don’t hold any grudges against you. Neither any of us to each other.” Bakushin could only scratch the back of her head as her lips turned back up into a slight grin. While it was true that Bakushin’s retirement was something she could look fondly on, it wasn’t without certain regrets that crept up on the brunette’s mind from time to time.
While growing up, it was umamusume like her, Chiyono O, Chitose O, and Laurel. Inheriting the positions of their mothers before them, this era of the Victory Club was a group of friends that made a promise to each other underneath the sakura blossoms of Springtime: with their strength, the four sought to not only support each other’s respective careers, but to bring their spring-blooming energy across the umamusume racing world to inspire others far and wide. These friends would prop each other up during the struggles of each of their own respective Twinkle series careers. Whether it be Laurel’s chronic leg disease, Chitose’s frequent sicknesses, Chiyo’s earlier than expected decline, and Bakushin’s lengthy hiatus due to injury after her first Sprinter’s Stakes, each one had their hurdle

The Sword Instructor's Report
by Eitan
The thing about teaching a man to use a greatsword is that you're teaching him to use his whole body as a lever, and if he gets it wrong, the lever breaks. I don't mean metaphorically. I mean the humerus, the clavicle, the cluster of small bones in the wrist that I couldn't name without a chirurgeon's manual but which I have felt give way under my own hands on three occasions, twice in training and once in what I suppose we have to call combat, though calling it combat lends it a dignity the event did not possess. A boy swung wrong. He was fifteen. I heard the sound from across the yard before I heard him scream.
So when they told me I was being reassigned to Redmane Castle to oversee weapons training for General Radahn's infantry, my first question was not "what's the pay" or "how long is the posting" or even "why me," though all three were relevant. My first question was: how many students, and how many of them have used a greatsword before?
The answer was two hundred and six, and none.
I should explain what Redmane Castle is, or was, before the rot. It sits on a promontory overlooking the southern approach to Caelid, which at that time was scrubland and red clay and not the suppurating wound it later became. The castle itself is — well, it's a castle. Built for a general who could hold back the stars, which means the proportions are wrong in a way that makes your neck hurt. The doors are too tall. The ceilings are too high. The training yard was designed for a man who could crater flagstone just by landing from a jump, and for the rest of us it felt like drilling in a cathedral.
Radahn's personal guard were Cleanrot Knights on loan from his sister's retinue, supplemented by heavy cavalry and a core of career infantry who'd been with him since the conquest of Sellia. Good soldiers. Professional. The kind of men who keep their kit clean and their mouths shut and can march thirty miles on a biscuit and a complaint.
The two hundred and six were not those men.
The two hundred and six were new levies. Farm boys from the eastern settlements, miners from the crystal caves near Sellia, a handful of merchants' sons who'd been volunteered by their fathers for reasons I suspect had more to do with inheritance disputes than patriotism. None of them had held anything heavier than a hoe. Several of them were afraid of horses. One of them — I remember this vividly — asked me on the first day whether the sword went in the left hand or the right.
I told him it went in whichever hand he was about to lose.
That was cruel, and I regretted it immediately, because the boy's face collapsed in a way that told me he was already terrified and had been hoping the instructor would be kind. I was not kind. I was tired, and the posting had started badly — my quarters had no window, my predecessor had left no training records, and the quartermaster had issued me one hundred and seventy practice swords for two hundred and six students, which meant either thirty-six men would sit out every session or I'd have to run shifts, which meant twice the hours for the same pay, which was already insufficient. The Codex says — well, the Codex doesn't apply here, does it. I'm aware this is a different world with different rules and different scripture. But the principle holds. The principle always holds. You don't break a frightened person to teach them courage. You give them something small they can do correctly, and you build from there, and you don't raise your voice unless the building is on fire.
I did not follow the principle with that boy. I followed the version of myself that was irritated about the practice swords.
His name was Millicent. No — that was someone else. His name was Torvin, and he was seventeen, and by the end of six weeks he could execute a basic overhead cut without dislocating his own shoulder, which in context was an achievement roughly equivalent to building a bridge.
The greatsword, for those who haven't had the misfortune of carrying one, is a weapon that punishes inattention. A regular sword — a longsword, a sidearm, the kind of thing a knight carries as a backup — operates within the natural arc of a human arm. You swing it, it goes where your hand goes, the geometry is intuitive. A greatsword operates outside that arc. The blade is long enough and heavy enough that once it's in motion, your body has to reorganize itself around the weapon's momentum rather than the other way around. You don't swing a greatsword. You start it moving and then you negotiate with the physics of the thing about where it's going to end up.
This is why greatsword technique is fundamentally different from other swordsmanship. With a longsword, speed is an advantage. With a greatsword, speed is a liability. If you try to swing faster than the weapon wants to move, you lose control of the arc, which means you lose your balance, which means the enemy's next strike lands on a man who is falling. The technique — the actual, physical, mechanical technique — is about rhythm. You find the weapon's natural period of oscillation and you work within it. You let the blade reach the end of its arc, feel the moment where the momentum dies and reverses, and you redirect. You're not fighting with the sword. You're conducting it.
I tried to teach this to two hundred and six men who'd never held a greatsword and whose instinct, uniformly, was to swing as hard and fast as possible.
It went about as well as you'd expect.
The first week was triage. You cannot teach a man rhythm if he cannot stand properly, and roughly a third of them couldn't. Farm boys stand with their weight back on their heels because that's how you brace behind a plough. Miners crouch. Merchants' sons stand whatever way makes them look tallest, which is never the way that keeps them alive. I spent four days teaching footwork before anyone touched a blade.
The training yard had a particular quality of echo. Every impact rang off the high walls and came back doubled, so that a session with fifty pairs drilling sounded like a session with a hundred. The quartermaster's shortfall on practice swords meant I ran three shifts — dawn, midday, and the late session that caught the last hour of usable light.
Dawn was the best shift because the men were too tired to be clever. Midday was the worst because the heat off the red clay turned the yard into a kiln and I lost two men to fainting in the first week alone. The late session was mine. The light went copper and the echoes softened and the men who showed up for the third shift were the ones who wanted to be there rather than the ones who'd been told to. Selenne was always there. Torvin, surprisingly, was always there. Hrath was not. Hrath trained at dawn, completed his drills with mechanical precision, and spent his evenings somewhere I never thought to ask about.
I ate with the career soldiers most evenings. They were polite about it. The career soldiers in any army are polite to the training staff the way a shipwright is polite to the man who supplies his timber: you're not one of us, exactly, but we need what you produce, so have a seat and don't expect to be consulted on navigation. The Redmane veterans had a particular brand of competence that I recognized and respected — the kind that comes from serving a general who is literally holding the sky in place. It sharpens your professionalism when your commanding officer is doing something impossible every day before breakfast.
I never met Radahn. That surprises people when I tell them — you were posted at Redmane Castle and never met the general? I was posted at the training yard. The general was at the front, or in the war room, or at Sellia studying gravity magic so his horse wouldn't have to carry him. We operated in parallel orbits. I saw his shadow once, crossing the upper courtyard at dusk, and my first thought was that someone had left a siege engine out. My second thought was that whoever commanded this much loyalty from the career soldiers probably deserved it.
Six weeks in, I had sorted the two hundred and six into three groups, which is how you always sort them. The naturals — about twenty, men whose bodies understood leverage before their minds did, who could feel the blade's period in their first session and adjusted their footwork without being told. The adequates — about a hundred and forty, who could be trained to competence through repetition and would fight well enough in formation, where individual error is absorbed by collective structure. And the remainder — about forty-six — who were simply not built for this weapon, either physically or temperamentally, and who would be better served carrying a polearm or a shield or, in a few cases, a letter of resignation.
One of the twenty was a man named Hrath.
Hrath was not the best of the naturals. That distinction belonged to a quiet woman named Selenne who moved like water around a stone and who I later learned had been a dancer before the levies came. Hrath was the second-best, or possibly the third, depending on the day and whether Torvin was having one of his inexplicable good mornings. But Hrath had something the others didn't, which was an instinct for where the other person's body was weakest. Not an instinct for winning. An instinct for damage.
This is a distinction that matters. The best fighters in any army are the ones who understand structure — who read a formation the way an architect reads a building and find the point where one push will bring the whole thing down. They are efficient. They end fights quickly because they do not waste energy on inessential violence.
Hrath did not end fights quickly.
Hrath ended fights thoroughly. When I paired him against training partners, he didn't just land the scoring blow and reset. He followed through. He struck for the wrist after the point was made. He shifted his weight into the opponent's knee when the spar was already over. Small things. Things you might not notice if you weren't watching for them.
I was watching for them, because I have been teaching long enough to know that cruelty is a skill like any other, and it improves with practice.
I pulled him aside after the third incident. The conversation went approximately as follows. Me: "You're hitting after the point." Hrath: "I'm following through." Me: "The point is scored. The partner yields. The follow-through is unnecessary." Hrath: "In the field, nobody yields." Me: "This is not the field." Hrath: "Then what are we training for?"
Which is — I'll give him this — a fair question, and one that I did not have a good answer for, because the honest answer is: we are training you to do violence efficiently, but we would prefer you not enjoy it, and the distinction between efficient violence and enthusiastic violence is one of the many things I am paid to maintain despite the fact that in the actual moment of combat it is almost perfectly invisible.
I have had this conversation, or variations of it, with students in four different postings. It always goes the same way. The gifted cruel one asks why there are rules in training when there won't be rules in the field. The instructor explains that rules in training exist to produce soldiers, not casualties. The student nods, appears to understand, and then does the exact same thing in the next session, because the student was never confused about the rule. He was testing whether I'd enforce it.
I enforced it. I paired Hrath exclusively with the other naturals, who could defend themselves, and I told him that any further strikes after the scoring point would result in removal from the greatsword program. He stopped. Not because he agreed with me but because losing his position in the naturals would have been more painful to him than losing the extra hits. I had found his leverage. It was not the lever I wanted.
The thing is — and this is the part that McMeister doesn't adequately address, because McMeister was writing for a world that hadn't yet produced this particular problem — the thing is that Hrath's follow-through made him better. Not marginally. Measurably. In formation drills, the squad with Hrath in it consistently broke through opposing lines faster, because the opposing line knew what Hrath would do if they didn't yield quickly enough. His reputation preceded his sword. The other students gave ground rather than face him, which meant his squad advanced, which meant his squad won, which meant Hrath was, by any metric the army cared to measure, my best product.
McMeister writes — and again, I'm aware this is the wrong world, the wrong text, the wrong tradition, but the man got some things right regardless of context — McMeister writes that the purpose of martial training is not to create warriors but to create people who can wage war without losing the part of themselves that knows war is terrible. The sword, he says, is a tool. The warrior who treats it as an extension of his will is a craftsman. The warrior who treats it as an extension of his appetite is a danger to everyone, including himself.
Hrath was not a danger to himself. He was very careful. That's what worried me.
I continued training him because my orders were to train all two hundred and six, and because removing a gifted student from the program on the basis of my personal assessment of his character would have required paperwork I did not have the authority to file and a conversation with the general's staff that I did not want to have. Radahn's people did not share my concerns about the disposition of individual soldiers. They wanted bodies who could fight. The qualities that made Hrath troubling to me — his precision, his follow-through, his lack of wasted motion — were precisely the qualities that made him valuable to them.
So I trained him. I trained all of them. Six months. The greatsword form, the formation drills, the close-quarters adaptation for castle defense, the controlled retreat pattern that Radahn's strategists had developed for fighting in terrain where cavalry couldn't operate. I watched Hrath get better and better at a thing I increasingly wished he were worse at.
And then Malenia came.

Literally Trash Stage
Jabber was always an eccentric kid. From the moment he was brought into this world, he was treated as something precious and special. But had that ever been, truly, enough for him? He was only human. And humankind was naturally greedy; a person could do nothing but want more.
He was born in a facility with his mother. She cared for him as much as she could. The woman had long dark brown hair that the young boy loved to play with. She was always clad in ten silver rings. She did love him dearly, but such an innocent feeling was always limited in this world.
“Mama! Your rings are so beautiful. Where did you find them?” Jabber exclaimed, holding his mothers hands in his own. “Would you like to wear them?” The mother replied, gently pulling off each of hear silver rings. She began to put them on Jabber’s hands, as a gift to her beloved son. “Woah! Thank you, mama!” He giggled in delight, fanning his fingers apart to admire the rings. They were far too big on him, but maybe one day he’d grow into them.
Then, on one day, Jabber’s mother came back to him. She told the young boy that she had good news, and to listen carefully. “Mama is going to go away for a little while to make clothing.” She said, gently. Only, those words hurt more than anything else. “But… You’re gonna come back, right?” He replied, cautiously. “Of course.” The mother lied.
She was permitted to work by the segyeins, and could go outside to make and sell clothing, all while being provided for. She seemingly had a natural talent for fashion, and wanted to make the most of it. It was a lifelong dream that had finally become a success. But success could only get you so far.
She worked hard up until the day she’d gotten crushed. The segyein had come in Jabber’s room and pulled him out, though he was starting to cry. He did not know what was happening, and he could only assume the worst. They told him his mother was dead and was discarded. This memory had been long forgotten in Jabber’s mind, but it was one that made a lasting impact. They figured telling their pet the truth was cruel enough, but the segyeins were angry. There was no extra money coming in, and now their only female pet human was gone. 
He was always emotionally underdeveloped, but this only further stunted it. And so he was on his own for a while. He was dropped in a kind of slums, forced to fend for himself. Jabber was now deemed useless, if the provider was gone. He couldn’t reproduce, so there was no point in keeping him. He was just a waste in the world.
His despair in the slums had only lasted a couple days before a kind segyein noticed him. It never disclosed exactly what it was doing on that day, but it found Jabber and immediately took pity on him. There was some sort of gentleness in its actions.
“Are you lost, little creature?” It spoke, wistfully. Jabber could hardly hear it. He could only sit there and watch, not knowing what it was trying to get across. It asked a few more quiet questions, before giving up and taking the boy into its arms. “Come with me, child. I’ll give you a home.”
And so, Jabber was given life once again—in an ocean-like planet, where it was dark and safe. He had someone to love him. It was a gift to be loved, as a rotten human like himself. His short and mundane encounters with segyein had taught him that he was nothing short of a jester in their eyes. He had little to no respect for them because of that, but nor did he respect himself. For him, everyone was twisted and sick in their own ways. This cruel way of thinking only got worse as the years progressed.
The segyein treated him as if he were a pet. Always cooing over his every action. He didn’t care for satisfying it if it was completely fake. The constant attempts that the segyein made to get him to be happy backfired, and instead he grew to love pain. The lack of pain in his world had made him crave it. He loved the exhilarating feeling of something completely new, although it was fleeting. The only thing that could be better than that twisted rush of ecstasy, would be to inflict his pain on someone else.
Maybe in his mind, such a thing could be considered love.
His new caretaker had one day shown him a small video of some sort of contest. He was immediately so attached to the idea, almost obsessing over it. The way a person on screen got was shot fell to the ground in agony after losing a fair match. Maybe competing against someone strong, and finally getting hurt, would fill the empty void in his heart. So he begged and pleaded to go, going to drastic measures such as holding his breath and hurting himself on purpose. After all, if he couldn’t be crushed—crushing someone else would be the next best thing.
“Oh no, don’t do that, you’ll hurt yourself!” The segyein cried, attempting to coax Jabber out of his stubborn state. “… All right, I suppose you can go. Mommy only wants whats best for you…” It said earnestly, finally giving in. Jabber hadn’t felt such an overwhelming sense of joy in his life. He would no longer be trash.
For months, he waited. It was a slow, agonizing wait. He could hear his mother’s cries each time it registered that its beloved pet would be departing from it. Such a fragile love was bound to be broken. Could that really be called a love at all? Jabber did not know. What to even make of the word? He’d occasionally heard it in books, but it never truly meant anything to him. He only knew it was feeble, and it was best to avoid it.
“All right… I’m sure you’ll do amazing. Mommy will be watching you, my sweet Jabber.” She said. It was a bit out of place for a segyein to care about a pet human in this way.
With many heartbroken goodbyes from his segyein mother, he was sent off to the place called Anakt Garden in a clear glass box. Apparently, this was a learning environment based off of the planet his ancestors had lived on a long time ago. He was excited to feel stronger and powerful, prepared to find someone strong enough to crush him, or someone weak enough to crush. That’s his one true goal, and then he will have lived a fulfilling life.
Jabber was trapped for a long while, seemingly alone. The loving mother had given him blankets, and dressed him in warm clothes. It was slightly comforting. He was alone with his thoughts, and for the first time, he had hope for the future. He would be able to live out his only dream here. He could find a strong opponent, and get strong.
It had been at least an hour until he spotted something across from him. There was a smaller boy. He was barefoot, his glass box entirely empty. He had on puffy white clothes that looked suffocating, and sported an empty, barren look in his eyes. Almost doll-like. Jabber could only feel drawn to him. Could this be the opponent he was looking for? 
When their eyes met, Jabbers heart almost skipped a beat. It was such an unfamiliar feeling, it got him excited. He now had a big silly grin on his face as he looked over at the vacant-looking child. The other boys eyes wandered up to Jabbers face, curiously.
Their boxes then came to a stop, right next to each other. Jabber could only stare, until he reached out and put both of his hands on the surface of his glass box. The other boy copied the action. It made Jabber brighten with excitement. Just from seeing this boy up close...
Jabber was enchanted.
